text: The surgeon begins the procedure by making a medial peritoneal opening just above the level of the promontory, exposing the area for mesh fixation.

The surgeon identifies the left ureter and the superior rectal artery, highlighting these anatomical structures within the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects the left ureter, maintaining proximity to the rectum to avoid injury while dividing the left side of the peritoneal reflection.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection, encountering difficulty due to a fibrotic scar extending from the pouch of Douglas to the rectovaginal septum, visible from previous perineal procedures.

The second assistant applies additional counter-traction from inside the vagina, aiding in the identification of the correct dissection plane and minimizing the risk of injury to the rectal wall and vaginal vault.

The surgeon completes full rectal mobilization following posterior and lateral dissection, while preserving lateral stocks to maintain anatomical integrity.

The second assistant applies cephalic traction to the prolapsed rectum, allowing the surgeon to assess its complete reduction within the surgical field.

The surgeon performs sacral rectopexy by securing a polypropylene mesh strip to the lateral aspect of the lower rectum and the sacrum using two O-proline sutures on each side.

The surgeon carefully sutures the ends of the polypropylene mesh strip to the lateral aspect of the lower rectum, ensuring not to make full thickness bites in the rectum or cause injury to the ureters and iliac vessels.

The surgeon carefully sutures the ends of the polypropylene mesh to the rectum, ensuring that the sutures do not penetrate the full thickness of the rectum or cause injury to the ureters and iliac vessels.
The surgeon closes the pelvic peritoneal opening using two absorbable V-lock running sutures to prevent contact between the mesh and the small bowel.
